text: The surgeon tests the movement of wristed instruments, including a needle holder in the left hand and a cadier in the right, while attempting to push the diaphragm down as CO₂ is pressurized. The surgeon prepares to perform diaphragm placation stitches using Tuo Ethibond without pledgets, aiming to tighten the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to place the needle holder precisely on the needle for horizontal mattress interrupted sutures on the diaphragm. The first suture is placed at the center of the diaphragm, which is bowed towards the surgeon. The surgeon adjusts the CO₂ pressure to facilitate the procedure and uses the instruments to achieve the desired angle for needle insertion and extraction. The Cadi Air provides a gentle grip, while the needle holder offers a stronger grip. The surgeon adjusts the needle direction and places the knot on the anterior portion of the diaphragm, using a Chitwood knot pusher for efficiency.

The surgeon performs the second horizontal mattress stitch using a backhand technique, pulling the needle through the diaphragm tissue with a Cadia instrument. The needle holder is used to grasp the diaphragm, leaving visible marks on the tissue. The surgeon adjusts the needle to achieve a precise 90-degree angle in the holder, demonstrating the ease of positioning with wristed instruments.

The surgeon encounters minor bleeding while suturing the diaphragm, which has a rich vascular supply. A nasogastric tube is visible on the left side for stomach decompression. The right-handed instrument is used to create a ruffle in the diaphragm, ensuring no visceral organs like the spleen or stomach are behind it. The surgeon ties a Western knot to secure the suture.

The surgeon performs an instrument tie using wristed instruments, effectively holding the loop and throwing a knot. The video shows the placement of twelve horizontal mattress sutures to tighten the diaphragm during VATS placations. The pericardium is visible in the bottom right corner, and the surgeon ensures no structures are behind the diaphragm while suturing. The Cavidian EndoStitch device is used for diaphragm placations, shuttling the needle across efficiently.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to press on the diaphragm, assessing its tightness without CO₂ insufflation. A cyst located near the phrenic nerve is identified and unroofed using a Maryland bipolar instrument, which is effective in the mediastinal fat. The cyst is confirmed not to be a thymoma, and a needle is used to aspirate it for decompression.
